insights: Incidents are distributed with perfect uniformity across all five categories, with exactly 100 incidents (20% of total) per category, indicating no concentration, skew, or structural imbalance in category assignment.

The absence of any deviation from equal distribution across categories eliminates the presence of high-incidence categories, service bottlenecks tied to category volume, or resource allocation pressure arising from uneven demand.

All categorical fields—including 'assigned_to', 'assignment_group', 'location', and 'priority'—also exhibit full uniformity, confirming the dataset is synthetically balanced and contains no empirically grounded basis for disparity-based diagnostics.

This null result implies that category-level distribution cannot explain incident management challenges such as delays, backlogs, or performance variance—any observed operational issues must originate from dimensions other than category assignment.
summary: The analysis conclusively finds no discrepancy or imbalance in incident distribution across categories: each of the five categories accounts for exactly 20% (100 out of 500) of all incidents. This perfect uniformity extends to all other categorical fields examined, confirming the dataset is synthetically balanced and devoid of category-driven concentration or skew. As a result, category distribution cannot serve as an explanatory factor for operational disparities, risks, or inefficiencies—subsequent investigation should focus on non-categorical dimensions such as timing patterns, resolution duration, agent-level performance, or cross-category process variations.
